author: @CASHSEC
text: This is the type EV that should be readily available to everyone for the local runabout rather that the power hungry EV's being developed by car companies. So many political issues here. Then there are the legal issues relating to road use. Pressure is being applied to electric scooters. E push bikes are limited to 250 watt motors. So it goes on. Such a shame when we so need people like R M-S to allow these things to happen. E Bikes have gone to the wall when trying to mass produce these items. Suggest you come up with a home build.All weather, street legal version. People could buy the plans and build the kit. Then you might get hit with getting it certified as safe to use. I think I would start with a street legal trike and build up from there. What about creating a Website and recruit volunteers to do the research and design. Is this too complex. Keep doing what you do R M-S and enjoy your life as this is all that one needs.

author: @TechKnowCSS
text: Approximate top speed of your equad should be about 22 mph with a 500w motor, that motor has a rpm of about 2500, so the ratio from pinion to sprocket should be  8:1 for a 24" tire, OR 6.67:1 for a 20" tire. YOU ARE GEARED TOO HIGH and will fry the controller or motor or both, also will drain your batteries too fast.  You can over volt that motor to 36 volts and get 1000 watts and a rpm of 3750 that will bring your speed up to 33 mph. Electric motors have a max watt rating not max voltage and the wattage rating can be exceeded for short bursts with no damage, the max watts are set for continuous duty.  If your controller has all capacitors with voltage of 50v or more you can upgrade to 36v without a new controller because a 24v battery is really  30 volt system with 35 volt caps and a 36 volt battery is a 45v system needing 50 volt caps and a 48 volt battery needs 63 volt caps or higher. So if your controller has 35 volt caps you need to upgrade the caps to 50 volt of same capacity or get a new controller. There is also a controller HACK to get more power/speed/torque but you must be careful on the take-offs as you can fry your controller mosfets. Inside the controller is a SHUNT as like in a amp meter, just solder in a piece of metal over it. GOOD LUCK AND KEEP HAVING FUN ! ! !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - especially the advice on gearing

replies.author: @TechKnowCSS
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you for the props, so rare now days. I love all that you do, I love being a "MAKER" myself, Much respect for you.

replies.author: @TechKnowCSS
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering By the way, as for batteries there is a basic general estimate of about 1 mile per 1 amp hour (+ or -) .

replies.text: dunno mate I guess it kind of could

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
It absolutly could. But batteries are needed for general usefullnesd in speed distance and to operate at times when there's no or limited sun. 

Solar PV vs battery is not a valid dicotomy. They can work together for improving range and top speed. But the added usefulness of solar side is generally relativly very limited.  


Solar power carts have been  challenged in long distant races.  power translates to speed.  A solar cart without batteries sustained on  pv alone will only be able to go a certain distance per unit of time in a given light.  Adding a battery weighs the cart down reducing the velocity one can sustain on PV alone, but the battery provides power to go much faster for a limited duration, not to mention the fact that one starts their trip with the battery charged Batteries make a solar pv cart more pratical in terms of speed and distance, but the trip is started with batteries charged on solar after a long rest or charged more quickly with a plugged in battery charger.
